content.full_text:
February 25, 1992

Scott Hallabrin, General Counsel
Fair Political Practices Commission
428 J Street, Suite 800
Sacramento, CA 95814

Subject: CONFLICT OF INTEREST RE CONDOMINIUM CONVERSION ORDINANCE

Dear Mr. Hallabrin:

I would like to formally ask, pursuant to Cal.Reg.Code, tit. 2, section 18329, your opinion regarding whether certain Berkeley City Councilmembers have any conflict with regard to a proposed ordinance permitting conversion of rental units and TIC units to condominiums.

Since 1984, the City of Berkeley had conditioned condominium conversion on the vacancy rate for rental housing being in excess of 5%. That rate has never been exceeded. Recently, the Council enacted a moratorium on the creation of tenancy in common projects (TIC), a method of ownership that sought to avoid the condominium conversion restrictions. The TIC moratorium was the subject of an opinion from your office. (See copy attached for your convenience.) Now the Council is considering an ordinance which would permit the creation of TICs from rental units, and condominiums from rental units and TICs under certain conditions. We seek your opinion as to whether certain Councilmembers have a conflict of interest because they own property which may be impacted by the proposed legislation.

More specifically Councilmember Dean is a part owner of a duplex, 1344 Bonita Avenue, which has always been owner occupied, and is now occupied by her son. The other unit is rented but not subject to rent control. It would be eligible for conversion to condominiums or a TIC. Councilmember Chandler owns one unit of a two-unit TIC in which she lives, located at 1836 Cedar Street. It would be eligible for conversion to a condominium. Councilmember Wainwright owns seven four-plexes in Berkeley which she holds as rental property. They are located at 2758-64, 2774-80, 2750-56, 2782-2788 and 2790-2796 Sacramento Street,

Scott Hallabrin, General Counsel  
February 25, 1992  
Page 2

1235 Dwight Way and 2453 10th Street. They would be eligible for conversion to condominiums or TICs.

Understanding that your opinion would be based in part on a factual analysis, I asked the City's appraiser, Michael Yovino-Young for his opinion as to whether the above-mentioned properties would be affected by a new ordinance permitting condominium conversions and TIC conversions. His opinion is attached for your convenience. In brief he concludes no foreseeable significant financial impact on any of the above cited properties from the proposed new ordinance.

We would appreciate your opinion as to each of the Councilmembers as described in this letter. Thank you for your assistance.

Respectfully submitted,

MANUELA ALBUQUERQUE  
City Attorney

By  
MARJORIE GELB  
Assistant City Attorney

MG:dej

Attachments

cc: City Clerk
